solution: By the product rule, \( \log_b M + \log_b N = \log_b(MN) \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]]} \) and \( [[N]] = [[b]]^{[[q]]} \), the sum of the exponents is \( [[p]] + [[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two exponents; the product rule adds them.
This subtracts the exponents, which corresponds to a quotient, not a product.
This takes the log of the SUM of \( M \) and \( N \) rather than their product.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the quotient rule, \( \log_b M - \log_b N = \log_b(M/N) \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]]} \) and \( [[N]] = [[b]]^{[[q]]} \), the difference of the exponents is \( [[p]] - [[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two exponents instead of subtracting them.
This adds the exponents, which corresponds to a product, not a quotient.
This takes the log of the DIFFERENCE of \( M \) and \( N \) rather than their quotient.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the power rule, \( \log_b(M^{k}) = k\log_b M \). Since \( \log_{[[b]]}[[M]] = [[p]] \), the answer is \( [[k]]\cdot[[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the exponent \( k \) instead of multiplying it with \( \log_b M \).
This reports only the outer exponent \( k \), ignoring \( \log_b M = [[p]] \).
This reports only \( \log_b M \), forgetting to multiply by the outer exponent \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the change-of-base formula with common base \( [[c]] \), \( \log_{[[a]]}([[M]]) = \dfrac{\log_{[[c]]}([[M]])}{\log_{[[c]]}([[a]])} = \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, dividing the base's exponent by the argument's instead of the reverse.
This subtracts the exponents, as if using the quotient rule instead of changing base.
This multiplies the exponents instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is \( b^{d} \), the value of \( x-c \); you still must add \( c \).
This uses \( b\times d \) instead of \( b^{d} \) when undoing the logarithm.
This subtracts \( c \) instead of adding it.
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by two from the correct solution.

stem: Solve \( \log_{[[b]]}(x) + \log_{[[b]]}(x + ([[a]])) = [[k]] \) for \( x \). (The equation has only one solution where both logarithms are defined.)
solution: By the product rule, \( \log_{[[b]]}\big(x(x+([[a]]))\big) = [[k]] \), so \( x(x+([[a]])) = [[b]]^{[[k]]} \). This quadratic has roots \( x = [[b]]^{[[i]]} \) and \( x = -[[b]]^{[[j]]} \); the negative root makes \( \log_{[[b]]}(x) \) undefined, so the only valid solution is \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the right-hand side \( b^{k} \), the product \( x(x+a) \) itself, not \( x \).
This is the negative, extraneous root excluded by the logarithm's domain.
This is the magnitude of the extraneous root, not the valid solution.
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by two from the correct solution.

stem: Solve \( \log_{[[b]]}\!\left(x^{2} - ([[D]])\right) = [[k]] \). Which of the following is a solution?
distractor_rationales: This reports \( b^{k} \), the value of \( x^{2}-D \), rather than \( x \) itself.
This reports the shift constant \( D \) rather than solving for \( x \).
This reports \( x^{2} \) itself rather than taking the square root.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by one from a genuine solution.
This is off by two from a genuine solution.

solution: By the quotient rule, \( \log_{[[b]]}\!\left(\dfrac{x+[[a]]}{x}\right) = [[k]] \), so \( \dfrac{x+[[a]]}{x} = [[b]]^{[[k]]} \). Clearing the fraction gives \( [[a]] = x([[b]]^{[[k]]}-1) \), so \( x = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]^{[[k]]}-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the constant \( a \) itself instead of solving for \( x \).
This divides \( a \) by \( b^{k} \) instead of by \( b^{k}-1 \).
This multiplies instead of dividing when isolating \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by one from the correct solution.
This is off by two from the correct solution.

stem: Solve \( \log_{x}([[N]]) = [[k]] \) for the base \( x \) (with \( x > 0, x \ne 1 \)).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( k \) instead of taking the \( k \)-th root.
This subtracts \( k \) instead of taking the \( k \)-th root.
This reports the exponent \( k \) itself instead of the base \( x \).
This raises \( N \) to the power \( k \) again instead of taking its \( k \)-th root.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: The natural logarithm and \( e^{x} \) are inverse functions, so \( \ln(e^{[[k]]}) = [[k]] = [[answer]] \) directly.
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This leaves the expression unevaluated instead of applying the inverse property.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.
